name: Bhartrhari
dates_notes: Dated by the Chinese pilgrim I-tsing's reference; the Vākyapadīya survives.
core_thesis: Bhartṛhari is a grammarian-philosopher in the Pāṇinian tradition, and his thesis is named with care: *śabda-advaita*, word-non-dualism. The ultimate is *śabda-brahman* — an undivided word-principle from which the manifest world of meanings, objects, and cognitions arises by *vivarta* (apparent transformation, not real modification). The opening verse of the *Vākyapadīya* fixes this: *anādi-nidhanaṃ brahma śabda-tattvaṃ yad akṣaram / vivartate 'rtha-bhāvena prakriyā jagato yataḥ* — "That imperishable word-reality, *brahman*, beginningless and endless, appears in the form of objects; from it the world-process [proceeds]." The verb *vivartate* is load-bearing: appearance, not transformation. A second thesis follows. There is no cognition (*pratyaya*) unsuffused by *śabda*; awareness is intrinsically linguistic. A third thesis concerns the units of meaning. The integral *vākya* (sentence) is meaning-bearing and yields a single instantaneous *pratibhā* (intuitive flash); the analyzed *pada* and *varṇa* are abstractions extracted by grammatical analysis, not the basic semantic atoms. A fourth thesis orders the descent of speech: *paśyantī* (seeing), *madhyamā* (intermediate, mental), *vaikharī* (articulated). The work is grammatical in genre, but its *vivarta* idiom and its monistic word-ontology pass directly into Maṇḍana and from Maṇḍana into the technical Advaita lexicon.

engaged_works.title: Vakyapadiya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; engaged here only insofar as it is Vedāntic in implication, especially the śabda-brahman doctrine that influences Maṇḍana.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus on Bhartṛhari's authorship of the Vākyapadīya.

engaged_works.summary: A *prakaraṇa* in three *kāṇḍas* — hence the alternative title *Trikāṇḍī*. Each *kāṇḍa* takes one stratum of grammatical philosophy. *Brahma-Kāṇḍa* (also *Āgama-Samuccaya*) develops the metaphysics of *śabda-brahman*, the *sphoṭa* doctrine of meaning-bearing linguistic units, and the three levels of *vāk*. *Vākya-Kāṇḍa* defends the primacy of the integral sentence over its analytic parts, surveys competing theories of meaning, and gives the doctrine of *pratibhā* (the unified meaning-flash). *Pada-Kāṇḍa* (also *Prakīrṇaka*) analyzes nominal categories one at a time: *jāti*, *dravya*, *saṃbandha*, *sādhana*, *kriyā*, *kāla*, *puruṣa*, *saṃkhyā*. Bhartṛhari's own *vṛtti* survives on *kāṇḍas* 1 and 2; its authorship on *kāṇḍa* 1 is contested (Aklujkar treats it as Bhartṛhari's own; others remain skeptical). The work matters for Vedānta on two specific points. The *vivarta* terminology — *brahman* appearing as the manifold without itself undergoing real change — gives Maṇḍana and the later Advaitins their preferred causal idiom against *pariṇāma*. The conception of an undifferentiated linguistic absolute grounding all cognitive content gives them a model for *brahman* as the ground of *pramāṇa*-activity. The bulk of the work is grammatical-philosophical and outside the strict Vedāntic *prasthāna*; it is engaged here for its Vedāntic implication only.
